records.ety: [Orig. the preterit of the verb to owe. OE. oughte, aughte, ahte, AS. āhte. √110. See Owe.]
usage: Both words imply obligation, but ought is the stronger. Should may imply merely an obligation of propriety, expendiency, etc.; ought denotes an obligation of duty.

defs: Was or were under obligation to pay; owed.
quotes: This due obedience which they ought to the king.
The love and duty I long have ought you.
[He] said . . . you ought him a thousand pound.
The knight the which that castle ought.

defs: To be bound in duty or by moral obligation.
quotes: We then that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak.

defs: To be necessary, fit, becoming, or expedient; to behoove; -- in this sense formerly sometimes used impersonally or without a subject expressed.
quotes: To speak of this as it ought, would ask a volume.
Ought not Christ to have suffered these things?
notes: ☞ Ought is now chiefly employed as an auxiliary verb, expressing fitness, expediency, propriety, moral obligation, or the like, in the action or state indicated by the principal verb.
